title: The Christian Ministry: 1 Corinthians 3:5-15
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Christian Ministry_ 1 Corinthians 3_5-15 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: The Christian Ministry is an Instrument of God (vv. 5-7)
The Christian Ministry is a Coworker with God (v. 9) — illustrated by farming (vv. 6-9) and building (vv. 9-11)
The Christian Ministry is Accountable to God (vv. 12-15)
summary: Preaching from 1 Corinthians 3:5-15 in a Sunday evening service, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses the Corinthians' immature boasting over which minister saved them. He shows that ministers are mere instruments in God's hands — nothing in themselves — who work alongside God as farmers sow and water and builders lay on the one foundation, Jesus Christ. The sermon closes by warning that every minister's work will be tested by fire on the day of judgment, and calls all Christians to examine whether they are instruments of God's glory or self-promoting prima donnas in their own lives.
transcript:
In this section of 1 Corinthians, we see that at least some of the Christians in the Corinthian church were childish in their faith at a time when they should have been mature. The evidence Paul brings that displays their immaturity is that they were ruled by envy and strife. One of the areas where they clashed with one another was the question of under whose ministry they were saved. Somehow they thought that if they were saved under this pastor, that made them more important than someone saved under another pastor — a kind of "whose daddy is bigger" contest: "I was saved under Paul, therefore I'm more important." "I was saved under Apollos, so I'm more important than you." In that competition, they showed a great misunderstanding of the Christian ministry.

In our passage, Paul corrects their view. He wants us to see three things about the Christian ministry: it is an instrument of God, a coworker with God, and accountable to God.

## The Christian Ministry Is an Instrument of God

We see this in verses 5 through 7. Paul clarifies who these men they had been idolizing really are. Interestingly, the original language of verse 5 does not even use the word "who" — it uses a word closer to "what." Paul does not even raise himself to the level of a person: *What is Paul? What is Apollos?* Just instruments of God.

Paul shows that these men, including himself, are not where Christians should find their identity. Who are these men you have been boasting about? What is in them that should cause you to boast in them? They are simply servants through whom you believed.

It is interesting that Paul often used the word "servant" to refer to himself, and more often used the same word as "slave." In Romans 1:1 he says, "Paul, a bond-servant of Jesus" — a slave of Jesus. But here he uses a different word: a servant less than a slave, the word that eventually became the technical term for *deacon*. Originally it described a servant who made less than a slave and yet willingly served anyway — a position of a servant with less standing in the household and yet he served nonetheless. That is how Paul sees himself: the lowest of servants, the servant of the Lord Jesus Christ.

So Paul says these men were agents of God through whom you believed. You did not believe in Paul. You did not believe in Apollos. You believed in Jesus and were saved, regardless of who shared the gospel with you. That must be the attitude of every Christian minister. The ministry is not about themselves but about Christ. They are instruments in the hands of Christ. Their desire is that the people placed in their care would see and believe in Christ through them. They understand that it is not they who bring salvation — it is God who saves.

In verse 6, Paul says, "I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase." The Christian minister knows that he is nothing. In verse 7, Paul says, "neither he who plants is anything nor he who waters." Nothing. That is the word Paul uses.

Now the office of minister is an important one — Paul says it is worthy of double honor, especially for those who labor in word and doctrine. But it is not because of the office itself or the man who occupies it. It is because through the Christian minister God brings His word to His people. So the Christian minister should not seek to promote himself. He should not become famous. There should be no such thing as a celebrity pastor — at least, the pastor himself should never consider that to be the case, because the pastor is here to make Christ famous, not himself. His attitude must be that of John the Baptist: "I must decrease so that Christ might increase." Every minister of the gospel must have this attitude: I want to disappear so that people can see Christ.

But this is also true of everyone in the church. Everyone who professes faith in Jesus Christ is a servant of Jesus Christ, just as Paul was a *diakonos* of Jesus Christ. We see this in John 13, where Jesus says we are to wash each other's feet. Every Christian will minister in some capacity, so every Christian must think of themselves as nothing more than a tool in the hands of God.

A tool does absolutely nothing unless it is in the hands of a master craftsman. In fact, a tool can inflict great damage when used by someone who does not know how to use it or for the wrong purpose. A hammer is great for driving nails — that is what it does best. But it is terrible at everything else. A Christian excels at exalting and glorifying Christ. But we are all lousy self-promoters because we are made to glorify Christ.

Because this is true, there is no room for prima donnas in the church of the Lord Jesus Christ. The term "prima donna" comes from the world of opera — the woman who sang the most important part. The word became a term for the person who thinks they are the best thing and that everybody needs to cater to them. There is no room for that in the church of Jesus Christ.

## The Christian Ministry Is a Coworker with God

We see this in verse 9. Paul says, "For we are God's fellow workers; you are God's field, you are God's building." Some scholars suggest Paul means that Christian ministers are coworkers with each other as they serve God. But the more natural interpretation is that the Christian minister works together with God — is a coworker with God. Christian ministers are means through whom God works. And this is also true of everyone who professes faith in Christ.

Paul now describes how the Christian minister is a coworker with God using two analogies: farming and building.

### Farming God's Harvest (vv. 6–9)

Though it is God who gives the increase, the sowing and watering are still necessary. The one who plants is not better than the one who waters, or vice versa — they are one in the work. That is how the Christian life often goes: somebody plants something in your life, then somebody else waters that seed, and eventually you see fruit. Sometimes this is very evident in how we are brought to saving faith — somebody says a word here, somebody else says a word there, somebody else waters that word, until the day when our eyes are opened and we see the glory of Christ.

Though the sowing and watering are necessary, God is the one who brings the fruit. In the parable in Mark 4, Jesus says the farmer plants, goes to bed, and does absolutely nothing — and the seed germinates and brings forth fruit. That is how the gospel works.

So let me ask you: in what ways have you been a sower or a waterer? Spiritual sowing and watering may be discouraging because you may never see the fruit of it. But God says there will be fruit.

There is this account of an old man who lived in colonial Virginia, a man named Luke Short. He was 103 years old, sitting by a hedge, and remembered a sermon he had heard preached by John Flavel — the well-known Puritan pastor who died in 1691. As he recalled that sermon, God right then and there led him to seek forgiveness of his sins through Jesus Christ. Mr. Short then lived three more years, and when he died at age 106, his tombstone read: "Here lies a babe in grace, aged three years, who died according to nature aged 106." Mr. Short remembered a sermon he had heard preached by Flavel 85 years before he was saved — that was what brought him to faith in Christ. There is fruit even if we do not see it.

### Building God's Church (vv. 9–11)

The church of Jesus Christ is not only a cultivated field but also a building that is being built. The foundation of that building is Jesus Christ, as Paul says in verse 10: "According to the grace of God which was given to me, as a wise master builder I have laid the foundation, and another builds on it. But let each one take heed how he builds on it. For no other foundation can anyone lay than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ."

The Christian minister must have Jesus Christ and Him crucified as the focus of his ministry. The moment that is no longer the focus, it is time to find another minister. There is no other foundation that will sustain the church. Paul tells the Galatians that if anyone — even an apostle, even an angel from heaven — comes preaching another gospel, let him be accursed.

So the Christian minister must build the church on the foundation of Jesus Christ, and build carefully with the best materials. In verses 10 and 12, Paul likely had the teachers of worldly wisdom in mind when he speaks of building materials. Builders on the right foundation are using either non-combustible materials — gold, silver, precious stones that resist fire — or combustible materials — wood, hay, straw — that will perish on the day of judgment.

At the end of the day, two things will determine what type of material we use: Is the Bible the construction manual? And for whose glory are we building the church? Those two things will determine whether our work stands or falls on the day of judgment.

## The Christian Ministry Is Accountable to God

We see this in verses 12 through 15. Remember the Corinthians were fighting about who the better pastor was — Paul, Cephas, Apollos, Jesus. And Paul says: do not worry about who the best pastor is; that recognition will come on the day of judgment. Christ will vindicate those who are His and declare those who have failed as well.

This is a warning to every Christian minister: everything done in the church of Jesus Christ will be tested on the day of judgment. If the work endures the fire, the minister will be rewarded. If it does not, he will suffer loss. Interestingly, this does not mean the minister is not a Christian — both of them make it to heaven, the one who used gold and silver and the one who used straw, because they both built on the right foundation. But one will be saved as though through fire, smelling of smoke, as Paul says in verse 15.

This accountability is also true of every person who professes faith in Christ. You will be accountable for everything you do, every word you have spoken, every thought you have had, on the day of judgment. That should be very sobering to us.

## Three Action Points

So the Christian ministry is an instrument in God's hand for the good of the church — a coworker with God, and accountable to God. So are all of us. How do we live that out?

**First**, look at your life and examine whether you are functioning as an instrument in God's hands or as the main actor. Are you someone God is using for His glory, or are you living as the central figure, the prima donna of your own life? If you have been the main actor in your life and relationships, repent of that and start being used by God. You cannot be both an instrument and the main act.

**Second**, you are sowing and watering whether you realize it or not. What type of seed have you been sowing throughout your life? What fruit will what you have been sowing produce? Purposely seek to sow godly seed or water godly seed in the lives of those around you.

**Third**, you are a builder. What materials are you using in building the church and your life in Christ? Are you using prime materials, or is what you have for Christ just leftovers? A very concrete way to examine this is your giving — do you give to the kingdom of Christ financially from the top, or from what is left over? But then also look at your calendar, because time is our most precious commodity. Money we can make more of; time we cannot get back. How are you using your time to build God's kingdom? Your calendar is really the best testimony about your heart.

You are an instrument. You are sowing and watering. You are a builder. All of that for the glory of God and the good of His church.
